Overview: The CIA under Trump-era Director Mike Pompeo planned aggressive operations against Julian Assange and WikiLeaks following the 2017 Vault 7 leak, including surveillance, cyber theft, and discussions of abduction from the Ecuadorian Embassy in London, with internal talk of assassination never forwarded to the White House; these plans faced legal pushback from NSC lawyers and spurred a fast-tracked DOJ indictment, but were not executed amid fears of Russian extraction and British objections to violence, ultimately leading to Assange's 2019 embassy expulsion, UK arrest, and 2024 US extradition plea deal resolving espionage charges without trial.

- WikiLeaks published Vault 7 in 2017, exposing CIA hacking tools, deemed 'largest data loss in CIA history' by agency officials.

  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high

  source: Democracy Now (Yahoo News report)
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high

  source: Democracy Now (Yahoo News report)
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- NSC lawyers objected to abduction legality absent indictment, prompting DOJ to accelerate Assange charges in late 2017.
  source: Democracy Now (Yahoo News report)
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- Plans ramped up over credible intel of Russian plot to extract Assange to Moscow; included scenarios of London shootout, but Brits insisted on handling any violence.
  source: Democracy Now (Yahoo News report)
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- Assange expelled from embassy in 2019, arrested by UK on US warrant, faced extradition until 2024 plea deal on one Espionage Act count with time served.
  source: Wikipedia (contextual resolution)
  url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julian_Assange
  confidence: high

- CIA's aggressive 'vendetta' driven by Vault 7 embarrassment clashed with legal/doctrinal limits, as NSC/White House lawyers blocked rendition absent indictment, exposing institutional checks masking deeper power plays.
- Pompeo's framing of WikiLeaks as 'hostile non-state intelligence service' justified extremes, yet assassination talk stayed internal while surveillance succeeded, revealing selective incompetence or deliberate restraint for image management.
- Fear of Russian extraction escalated plans to potential gun battle in allied London, but British veto on US shooting highlighted sovereignty tensions and hidden incentives to pivot to legal routes over dramatic failure.
Broader system: Post-Snowden/Trump-era intelligence overreach where leaks trigger personal vendettas (Pompeo revenge) within vast surveillance state, bounded by inter-agency friction, allied constraints, and judicial backstops that convert assassination plots into protracted legal sieges, preserving institutional power through softer coercion while avoiding public scandal.

- Assange/WikiLeaks rose via 2010 Manning leaks (Collateral Murder, war logs, cables), escalating US enmity through 2016 DNC emails, setting Assange as symbol of transparency threat.
- Vault 7 dump in 2017 as 'largest CIA data loss' ignites Pompeo's war declaration, framing WikiLeaks as enemy to unlock covert ops amid Trump admin chaos.
- Assange's 2012 embassy refuge amid Swedish allegations (dropped 2017) creates pressure-cooker: spied on by UC Global (CIA-linked), isolated, with Russian extraction intel building to operational climax.
Actual resolution mechanism: Assassination/abduction plots abandoned amid legal/allied pushback; CIA secured leverage via embassy surveillance feeding DOJ case, Ecuador's 2019 expulsion under US pressure, UK arrest, years of bail/extradition battles, culminating in 2024 plea deal granting Assange freedom after time served—system neutralized threat quietly through lawfare, not bullets.

- Did CIA intel on Russian extraction serve as pretext to escalate or genuine trigger, and why no follow-up probes into Pompeo's internal assassination talks?
- Extent of UC Global's role: confirmed spying, but unproven kidnapping prep—how deep was Spanish firm's US ties?
- Post-plea: Assange's silenced voice as hidden win, or does it signal institutional drift toward normalizing journalist prosecutions?

- Unresolved Shadows: Press Freedom's Quiet Assassination: Broader system critique, open questions on incentives/power, sharpening Moon's contrarian edge.
